title: Cat Facial Droop: Causes, Signs, and Triage Advice
meta_desc: If your cat has a drooping face, it could be Horner's Syndrome or nerve issues. Learn about the symptoms and when to contact your veterinarian.
content_html:
TL;DR: Facial drooping in cats is usually caused by nerve issues or infections and requires a veterinary exam within 24 hours, though immediate emergency care is needed if it is accompanied by a head tilt or difficulty walking.

What is facial drooping in cats and what causes it?

Facial drooping, or facial paralysis, occurs when the nerves controlling the muscles of the face—typically the facial nerve or the sympathetic nervous system—are compromised. You may notice one side of the lip sagging, a droopy eyelid, or the third eyelid protruding. This is often associated with conditions like Horner’s Syndrome, middle ear infections, or idiopathic facial paralysis.

When is facial drooping in my cat considered a medical emergency?

  • The urgency level for facial drooping is generally Medium, requiring a veterinary examination within 24 hours.
  • While a drooping face is rarely life-threatening on its own, it indicates an underlying neurological or inflammatory issue.
  • You should seek immediate emergency care if the drooping is accompanied by a head tilt, inability to walk, or sudden trauma.

How can taking a photo of my cat help with the triage process?

  • A clear, front-facing photo allows the vet to see the exact degree of asymmetry, including ptosis (droopy eyelid) or miosis (constricted pupil).
  • Because these symptoms can sometimes shift or be subtle during a physical exam, a visual record helps the medical team track the progression and severity.
  • The photo helps the clinic assess the condition before you even arrive, ensuring better preparation for your visit.

Clinical Context (Merck Veterinary Manual)

Idiopathic facial paralysis in cats results in unilateral or bilateral paresis or paralysis of the facial muscles, with domestic longhaired cats at increased risk. Clinical signs include the acute onset of inability to blink, drooping ear, drooping upper lip, and drooling, although facial sensation remains intact. Other causes of facial paralysis, such as ear disease, trauma, and brain-stem lesions, should be excluded. Primary neoplasia affecting middle ear structures, such as squamous cell carcinoma and polyps, can also cause facial paralysis in cats. Otoscopy with biopsy and histologic examination is recommended. A lowered eyelid and deviated muzzle may also be present.

Chapter: Neurology

Source: The Merck Veterinary Manual, 11th Edition (Page 1239)

faqs.question: Can an ear infection cause a cat's face to droop?
faqs.answer: Yes, deep middle or inner ear infections can inflame the nearby facial nerves, leading to drooping or paralysis on the affected side.

faqs.question: What are common signs of Horner's Syndrome in cats?
faqs.answer: Common signs include a constricted pupil, a drooping upper eyelid, the eye appearing sunken into the socket, and the third eyelid being visible.

faqs.question: Is facial paralysis in cats permanent?
faqs.answer: It depends on the cause. If the underlying issue is treated, such as an infection, some cats regain function. In idiopathic cases, some drooping may remain permanent but typically does not affect the cat's quality of life.
